# Episode 8: Examples of Using Container Images in Practice {.nonincremental}

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" .nonincremental}
## Questions

- How can I use Docker for my own work?
:::

::: {.callout-tip icon="false" .nonincremental}
## Objectives

- Use existing container images and Docker in a research project.
:::

---

## Practical Workflow Examples

Practical workflow examples:

- Example 1: automate image publishing with GitHub Actions.
- Example 2: automate website deployment with GitHub Actions.
- Example 3: move container-based workflows onto HPC systems.

## GitHub Actions

GitHub Actions automates repetitive project tasks:

- Testing if your software works correctly (Continuous Integration)
- Building components for distribution to your users (Continuous Deployment)
- Building documentation

Why run this in the cloud?

- Your software works on your computer, but you forgot to mention this one crucial package that   is required for correct operation.
- Your software used to work, but since the last update something broke.
- Someone else contributed to your package but didn't run the same version of the document
  converter: the documentation looks different now.


## Example 1 - Automate Image Publishing with GitHub Actions

GitHub Actions can build a Docker image and publish it to Docker Hub automatically whenever you push to your repository.

Typical workflow:

1. Create or clone a GitHub repository
2. Add a `Dockerfile`
3. Add Docker Hub credentials as GitHub secrets
4. Add a workflow file
5. Push to `main` to trigger the build

---

## Step 1: Create or Clone a Repository

Clone an existing repository:

```bash
git clone https://github.com/Sanduni-P/publish-docker-images.git
cd publish-docker-images
```

Or create a new repository locally:

```bash
mkdir publish-docker-images
cd publish-docker-images
git init
```

If you create a new repository locally, also create an empty repository on GitHub.
You will push your files there later, and that is where you will add the Actions secrets.

---

## Step 2: Add a `Dockerfile`

Add a `Dockerfile` in the repository root.

```dockerfile
FROM alpine
RUN apk add --update python3 py3-pip python3-dev
CMD ["python3", "--version"]
```

This is the image definition that GitHub Actions will build and publish.

---

## Step 3: Create a Docker Hub Access Token

First, log in to Docker Hub in your browser.

Then create a personal access token:

- open your profile settings;
- go to Personal access tokens;
- create a new token such as `github-actions`;
- give it Read and Write permissions;
- copy the token and store it safely.

We use a token instead of a password so the workflow can authenticate securely.

---

## Step 4: Add GitHub Repository Secrets

In your GitHub repository:

- open **Settings**;
- go to **Secrets and variables** -> **Actions** -> **New repository secret**;
- add `DOCKERHUB_USERNAME`;
- add `DOCKERHUB_TOKEN`.

These secrets are available to the workflow at runtime and keep credentials out of the repository.

---

## Step 5: Add the Workflow File

Create `.github/workflows/docker-publish.yml`.

This file is written in YAML.

- `name` gives the workflow a label in GitHub;
- `on` says when the workflow should run;
- `jobs` defines the work to perform;
- `steps` lists the actions executed in order.

---

## Workflow Example


Update `your-dockerhub-username` and `your-image-name` before using it.

```yaml
name: Publish Docker image

on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main

jobs:
  push_to_registry:
    name: Push Docker image to Docker Hub
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    permissions:
      packages: write
      contents: read
      attestations: write
      id-token: write
    steps:
      - name: Check out the repo
        uses: actions/checkout@v4

      - name: Log in to Docker Hub
        uses: docker/login-action@f4ef78c080cd8ba55a85445d5b36e214a81df20a
        with:
          username: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_USERNAME }}
          password: ${{ secrets.DOCKERHUB_TOKEN }}

      - name: Extract metadata (tags, labels) for Docker
        id: meta
        uses: docker/metadata-action@9ec57ed1fcdbf14dcef7dfbe97b2010124a938b7
        with:
          images: your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name
          tags: |
            type=raw,value=latest
            type=sha

      - name: Build and push Docker image
        id: push
        uses: docker/build-push-action@3b5e8027fcad23fda98b2e3ac259d8d67585f671
        with:
          context: .
          file: ./Dockerfile
          push: true
          tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }}
          labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}

      - name: Generate artifact attestation
        uses: actions/attest@v4
        with:
          subject-name: index.docker.io/your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name
          subject-digest: ${{ steps.push.outputs.digest }}
          push-to-registry: true
```

---

## What This Workflow Does

- Starts a fresh Ubuntu runner on GitHub.
- Checks out your repository.
- Logs in to Docker Hub using the stored secrets.
- Generates tags and labels for the image.
- Builds the image from your `Dockerfile`.
- Pushes the image to Docker Hub.
- Creates an attestation recording where the image came from.


---

## Step 6: Commit and Push

If you cloned an existing repository:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add Dockerfile and GitHub Actions workflow"
git push origin main
```

If you created a new local repository:

```bash
git add .
git commit -m "Add Dockerfile and GitHub Actions workflow"
git branch -M main
git remote add origin <your-github-repository-url>
git push -u origin main
```

Pushing to `main` triggers the workflow.

---

## Step 7: Check the Workflow Run

On GitHub:

- open the repository;
- go to the **Actions** tab;
- open the latest workflow run;
- confirm that the build and push steps completed successfully.

If it succeeds, your image should now be available on Docker Hub.

---

## Challenge

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Run Image Created from GitHub Actions

Can you run the container image that was just published?

:::

---

## Solution

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false" collapse="true"}

## Solution

Replace `your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name` with your Docker Hub ysername and the image name you just used:

```bash
docker container run your-dockerhub-username/your-image-name
```

:::



## Example 2: Building Content for Deployment via GitHub Pages with Pandoc

Containers can power cloud CI/CD workflows.

- automate repetitive tasks;
- keep build environments consistent;
- deploy simple project websites automatically.

### Goal: publish a simple GitHub Pages site from `README.md`.

- the README stays the single source of truth;
- Pandoc converts it to HTML;
- GitHub Actions rebuilds and republishes the page automatically.

Pandoc performs the Markdown-to-HTML conversion.

::: {.callout-note}

## Why Pandoc?

Pandoc is useful here because it supports:

- Supports citations (from BibTeX or CSL database)
- Rendered equations (using MathJax, optionally numbered)
- Code highlighting
- Highly customizable
  

:::

## Workflow

- start with a repository containing `README.md` on `main`;
- convert it to `index.html` using Pandoc;
- publish the built files to `gh-pages`.

## GitHub Actions: Example Repository

Example repository: [jhidding/readme-pages](https://github.com/jhidding/readme-pages)

![](../episodes/fig/github-main-branch.png){alt='A GitHub project with a README' width="90%"}

## Rendered GitHub Pages Output

Pandoc converts the README into a simple static site.

![](../episodes/fig/github-io-pages.png){alt='Rendered GitHub Pages' width="90%"}

## `gh-pages` Branch Contents

The `gh-pages` branch holds the published site.

![](../episodes/fig/github-gh-pages-branch.png){alt='gh-pages branch of the project' width="90%"}

## Why Only `index.html` and `.nojekyll`?

Only `index.html` and `.nojekyll` are needed.

- `.nojekyll` tells GitHub Pages to skip Jekyll processing.
- Pages then serves the branch content directly.

## Create a GitHub Project

Create a GitHub project with a short `README.md`:

- Log in to GitHub.
- Create a new repository.
- Clone it locally (GitHub shows the exact command).

```bash
git clone <your-repo-url>
cd <repo-name>
```

## Verify Generated HTML Output

Check the Pandoc container first:

```bash
docker container run pandoc/core --version
```

Convert `README.md` inside the container:

```bash
docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/tmp pandoc/core /tmp/README.md
```

```output
<h1 id="readme-pages">readme-pages</h1>
<p>Example for generating GitHub.io pages from Readme with Pandoc.</p>
```

The mount exposes your repository as `/tmp` inside the container, so Pandoc can read and write files on your machine.

To create a complete HTML document:

- Add `--standalone` flag for full document structure
- Redirect output to save as `index.html` in `build/` directory

```bash
mkdir -p build
docker container run --mount type=bind,source=${PWD},target=/tmp pandoc/core /tmp/README.md --standalone --output=/tmp/build/index.html
```

```output
[WARNING] This document format requires a nonempty <title> element.
  Defaulting to 'README' as the title.
  To specify a title, use 'title' in metadata or --metadata title="...".
```

To suppress the warning, add this header to `README.md` (Or pass `--metadata title="..."` on the command line): 

```yaml
---
title: Hello, Pandoc
---
```


## Using Pandoc to Create a Website

Check the output with:

```bash
cat build/index.html
```

```output
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1999/xhtml" lang="" xml:lang="">
<head>
  <meta charset="utf-8" />
... etc
```

Once the local flow works, automate it with GitHub Actions.

## Create the Workflow and Trigger

GitHub Actions can rebuild and publish on each commit.

In GitHub, navigate to your project and create a new workflow:

- Click **Actions** tab
- Skip workflow templates  
- Select **set up a workflow yourself**
- Configure in YAML format

Start with the workflow name:

```yaml
name: Deploy pages
```

Define trigger conditions, for example push to `main`:

```yaml
on:
  push:
    branches:
      - main
```

## Define Build Job Steps

Then define the job steps.

```yaml
jobs:
  # a free machine-readable name for this job
  deploy:
    # Set permissions for this specific job to enable it to write to the gh-pages branch
    permissions:
      contents: write
    # specify the base operating system
    runs-on: ubuntu-latest
    steps:
      # fetch the contents of the repository
      - name: Checkout repo content
        uses: actions/checkout@v2
      - name: Prepare build environment
        run: |   # multiple Bash commands follow
          mkdir -p build
          touch build/.nojekyll
```
## Add Pandoc and Deploy Steps

Add the containerized Pandoc step:

```yaml
      - name: Run pandoc
        # Always specify a version!
        uses: docker://pandoc/core:2.12
        with:
          args: >-  # multi-line argument
            --standalone
            --output=build/index.html
            README.md
      - name: Deploy on github pages
        # Use a third-party plugin to upload the content
        uses: JamesIves/github-pages-deploy-action@4.1.0
        with:
          branch: gh-pages
          folder: build
```

Key differences in GitHub Actions:

- No `--mount` flag needed; files placed automatically in correct location
- Last step uploads `build` directory to `gh-pages` branch

**Enable GitHub Pages:**

- In repository **Settings**, scroll to **GitHub Pages**
- Select root folder of `gh-pages` branch
- Deploy completes in seconds

## Using Containers on an HPC Cluster

Containers are also useful on shared research infrastructure:

- build and test locally;
- run at scale on a cluster later;
- keep the software environment consistent across both places.


**One catch:** many HPC systems do not allow Docker itself for security reasons. Instead, they often use Singularity, Apptainer, or Shifter, which can still consume Docker-built images.


::: {.callout-tip}

## Future Workshop: Apptainer Deep Dive

Detailed Apptainer usage will be covered in the next workshop.

:::

Apptainer is similar to Docker, but typically uses `.sif` image files and is often better suited to shared HPC systems.

### Apptainer vs Singularity

- Apptainer is the actively maintained continuation used on many modern HPC systems.
- Singularity appears in many existing tutorials and cluster docs.
- In practice, commands are very similar, so older Singularity references are still useful.


---

## Apptainer Command Line Interface

::: {.callout-note .challenge-callout icon="false" }

## Apptainer Command Line Interface

Like we did with Docker, try to work out what commands Apptainer has.
Which one do you think is the equivalent of `docker run`?
:::


---

## Solution

::: {.callout-note .solution-callout icon="false"  collapse="true"}

`apptainer run` behaves similarly to `docker run`, but as we will see, the arguments are somewhat different.
:::

### Running Docker Images with Apptainer

Apptainer uses its own image format, so Docker images are typically pulled/converted first:

```bash
apptainer pull docker://alice/alpine-python
```

This creates `alpine_python.sif` in your working directory.
Run it with:

```bash
apptainer run alpine_python.sif
```

### Apptainer Exec

To run a custom command, use `apptainer exec`:

```bash
apptainer exec alpine_python.sif python -c 'print(1 + 1)'
```
